46: pater, eis cheiras sou paratithemi to pneuma mou — Father, into your hands I commit my spirit — Luke's uniquely reported last word from the cross quotes Psalms 31:5 (the evening prayer of Jewish piety, recited before sleep as a commendation of the soul to God's keeping). The address Pater (Father) is distinctively Lukan (not in Mark's dereliction-cry; Matthew adds the Ps 22 form); even at death Jesus addresses God as Father, maintaining the intimacy of the Gethsemane prayer (Luke 22:42). The death is a trust-filled commendation rather than a defeat.
1: anestē hapan to plēthos -- "the whole multitude rose up" -- anestē is the same verb used for resurrection; Luke uses the unified assembly (hapan to plēthos) to signal corporate guilt and the gravity of the moment. The Sanhedrin collectively escorts Jesus to Pilate without delay: formal Jewish process has concluded.
2: Accusation triad before Pilate: (1) diastrephounta to ethnos hēmōn -- "subverting/misleading our nation" (diastrephō = perverting, twisting aside; used of false prophets); (2) kōluein phorous Kaisari didonai -- "forbidding giving taxes to Caesar" (a politically explosive charge, directly refuted by Luke 20:25); (3) legonta heauton Christon basilea einai -- claiming to be Christ-king. The charges are carefully framed for a Roman governor, translating religious categories into sedition.
3: Su ei ho basileus tōn Ioudāiōn? Pilate's central question. Jesus' response sy legeis ("you say so") is an indirect affirmation -- an idiom of qualified acknowledgment. He neither flatly denies nor loudly claims the title; the kingship he embodies exceeds Pilate's political categories. Compare John 18:36 where Jesus explains his kingdom is not from this world.
4: aitia -- the Greek legal term for a charge, cause of accusation, grounds for prosecution. Pilate's first innocence declaration: ouden heuriskō aition en tō anthrōpō toutō = "I find no cause/charge in this man." This begins Luke's triple innocence verdict (v4, v14, v22) plus Herod's concurrence (v15) -- a fourfold juridical clearing that makes the execution a gross miscarriage of justice by design.
5: epischyron -- "pressing insistently/vigorously" (only here in NT). The leaders intensify: anaseiōn ton laon = "stirring up the people" -- the same verb used of demagogues. They trace his ministry from Galilee to Jerusalem to establish ongoing agitation. Hearing "Galilee" triggers Pilate's Herod maneuver (v6).
6: Pilate's inquiry whether Jesus is a Galilean moves the case to Herod Antipas (tetrarch of Galilee and Perea), who happens to be in Jerusalem for the Passover. Luke's legal instinct: proper jurisdiction matters. This is the only episode in any Gospel where Jesus appears before Herod Antipas.
7: epempsen auton pros Hērōdēn -- sent him to Herod. Luke has carefully prepared this: Luke 3:1 (Herod introduced as tetrarch), Luke 9:7-9 (Herod hears of Jesus, is perplexed, wants to see him), Luke 13:31-32 (Jesus warned "go tell that fox"). Now the meeting happens -- and it is profoundly anticlimactic, which is the point.
8: echarē liān -- "rejoiced greatly" (liān is an intensifier). Herod has wanted to see Jesus for a long time (ex hikanō chronōu), hoping to witness a sign (sēmeion). This mirrors the demand of the "evil generation" in Luke 11:29 -- a sign-seeking that is spiritually bankrupt. Herod treats Jesus as entertainment rather than encountering him as Lord.
9: autōs de ouden apekrinato autō -- "but he answered him nothing." Jesus' silence before Herod echoes the Suffering Servant of Isaiah 53:7 ("he opened not his mouth"). He performs no sign, offers no word. To those who approach with cynical curiosity rather than genuine inquiry, the Word himself is silent. The contrast with Jesus' open teaching throughout Luke is stark.
10: eistēkeisan de hoi archiereis kai hoi grammateis -- the chief priests and scribes stand there, eutonōs katēgorountes autou = "accusing him vigorously/strenuously" (eutonōs = with full tension, at full stretch). Luke preserves the parallel with earlier Sanhedrin opposition (Luke 19:47, Luke 20:1) -- they are relentless, while Herod is merely amused.
11: exouthenēsas -- "treating with contempt, making of no account" (exouthenēō = set at nothing). Herod with his soldiers mocks Jesus and puts on him esthēta lampran -- "brilliant/shining apparel," a royal robe as mockery. The irony is dense: the king of kings is robed in ironic splendor by a petty tetrarch. Luke notes this is mock-investiture; the real king passes through.
12: egenonto de philoi -- "they became friends" (from being enemies). Luke alone records this reconciliation between Pilate and Herod over the shared experience of Jesus. Acts 4:27-28 quotes Psalm 2 and identifies both Herod and Pilate as fulfilling the kings who "conspire together against the Lord and his anointed." Their friendship is complicity.
13: Pilate assembles the chief priests, rulers, and people together -- a tripartite audience for his second innocence statement. The political stage is set: Pilate publicly addresses the verdict to the full representation of Jewish leadership and populace.
14: anakrinas enōpion hymōn -- "having examined before you" -- anakrino is the technical term for a judicial preliminary hearing. Pilate's finding: ouden heuron en tō anthrōpō toutō aition = no cause at all. This is his second declaration. The phrase hōn katēgoreite kat' autou = "of the things of which you accuse him" shows the formal legal language.
15: all' oud' Hērōdēs -- "and not even Herod" found cause. Pilate appeals to Herod's concurrence: anepempsen gar auton pros hēmas = "for he sent him back to us" (implying nothing capital was found). The four-fold verdict (Pilate x3 + Herod x1) reaches its peak here: the whole Roman-Herodian judicial apparatus declares Jesus innocent.
16: paideusas -- from paideuō, "to educate, discipline, correct," but in a penal context = "flog/chastise." Pilate's compromise offer: a flogging as warning (paideusis) followed by release. The verb's normal meaning (educate) makes this even more ironic -- the incarnate Wisdom who teaches all is offered a "lesson" by his judge. He repeats this offer in v22.
18: anekragon de pamplēthei -- "they cried out all together, with the whole crowd" -- pamplēthei (only here in NT) = all as one mass, all at once. The unified demand: aire touton, apoluson de hēmin ton Barabban = "Away with this one, release Barabbas to us." The crowd has made its verdict before Pilate can finish speaking.
19: hostis ēn dia stasin tina genethenēn en tē polei kai phonon -- Barabbas had been thrown in prison for insurrection and murder that took place in the city. Luke's description (dia stasin... kai phonon = because of insurrection and murder) is deliberately ironic in light of the charges against Jesus in v2 -- subverting the nation, claiming kingship. The guilty man goes free; the innocent is executed.
20: Pilate addresses them again (palin), wishing to release Jesus -- the imperfect ēthelen shows his sustained desire. His will is thwarted by the crowd, not by any finding of guilt. Luke underscores the injustice: the governor knows the right verdict but lacks the will to enforce it.
21: Staurou staurou auton -- "Crucify, crucify him!" The doubled imperative marks the escalation and urgency of the crowd's demand. They do not argue the charges; they simply insist on crucifixion. The repetition enacts the relentlessness that Pilate cannot resist.
22: Pilate's third declaration of innocence: ouden aition thanatou heuron en autō = "I found no cause of death in him." The legal term aition thanatou = ground for capital punishment. He offers again to chastise (paideusas) and release. The threefold verdict structure (Lukan artistry) mirrors the three-fold denial of Peter (Luke 22:54-62) -- innocent repeatedly declared, yet condemned; loyal repeatedly claimed, yet denied.
23: epekeinto phōnais megalais -- "they pressed with loud voices" -- epikeima = lying upon, pressing down; their voices were great and insistent. Katischuon = "they prevailed, were strong enough" (katischō used of the gates of Hades not prevailing against the church in Matthew 16:18). The crowd's insistence prevails over Pilate's judgment.
24: epekrine Pilatos genesthai to aitēma autōn -- "Pilate decided that their demand should be granted." The judicial irony: the judge rules in favor of the demand (aitēma = request/petition) rather than the evidence. Roman legal process is shown as broken by political pressure.
25: ton de Iēsoun paredōken tō thelēmati autōn -- "but Jesus he handed over to their will." The devastating climax of the trial narrative. Paredōken = "handed over" (paradidōmi, the word for betrayal, the word for Judas handing over Jesus). Tō thelēmati autōn = "to their will/desire" -- not to justice, not to Roman law, but to the crowd's will. Yet paradoxically this is also the divine will (Luke 22:42) accomplished through human sin.
26: epelambononto Simōnos tinos Kyrēnaios -- they seized Simon from Cyrene, erchomenon ap' agrou = "coming in from the countryside/fields." He was drafted, not a volunteer. The compelled cross-bearing of a bystander becomes the living illustration of Luke 9:23 ("take up your cross daily and follow me"). Luke adds epetithēsan autō ton stauron pherein opisthen tou Iēsou -- "placed on him the cross to carry behind Jesus" -- the disciple-following posture is physically enacted by this stranger.
27: kopetō kai thrēnō -- two types of mourning: kopetos = beating the breast in grief (from kopto = to strike/cut), thrēnos = a dirge or lamentation chant. The women's mourning is authentic grief, unlike the mockery of the soldiers. Luke's women (also Luke 8:2-3, Luke 23:49, Luke 23:55-56, Luke 24:1-10) witness every stage of the passion, providing unbroken testimony from cross to tomb to resurrection.
28: Thugateres Ierousalēm -- "Daughters of Jerusalem" -- a Semitic address form (cf. Song of Solomon, Lamentations). Jesus turns to them (strapheis) to redirect their grief: weep not for me but for yourselves and your children. The coming judgment on Jerusalem (Luke 19:41-44, Luke 21:20-24) will be so devastating that those without children will be counted blessed -- a shattering reversal of the highest Jewish blessing.
29: Makaria hai steirai -- "Blessed are the barren" -- stark reversal of the beatitude in Luke 1:25 (Elizabeth's barrenness removed) and normal Jewish expectation. Kai hai koiliai hai ouk egenнēsan = "and the wombs that never bore." When the siege comes, those with children will have more to lose, more to suffer. This is not praise of childlessness but a warning of the extremity coming upon Jerusalem.
30: Hosea 10:8 ("they will say to the mountains, 'Cover us,' and to the hills, 'Fall on us'") -- an appeal for the mercy of sudden death over prolonged suffering. The same verse appears in Revelation 6:16 in the eschatological judgment. Luke's Jesus applies the prophetic tradition to the coming Roman siege (70 CE).
31: hoti ei en tō hygrō xylō tauta poiousin, en tō xērō ti genētai? -- "For if they do these things in/to the green wood, what will happen in the dry?" Proverb form. Hygrōs = moist/green/living wood; xēros = dry/withered wood. Jesus is the living wood -- innocent, full of life. If Roman power and human sin can do this to him, what will happen when Jerusalem is dry, God-forsaken, ripe for destruction? The proverb asks the city to consider its own spiritual state.
32: Two others (kakourgoi = evildoers, criminals, a Lukan word for criminals in the passion narrative) are led out with Jesus to be executed -- the plural fulfills Isaiah 53:12 ("numbered with the transgressors"). Luke names them kakourgoi throughout rather than lēstai (robbers/bandits) as in Mark/Matthew.
33: Kranion -- the skull-place (Latin: Calvaria, hence "Calvary"). They crucified him there, and the criminals -- one on the right, one on the left. The crucifixion itself receives minimal description (only "they crucified him"), consistent with all the Gospels -- the physical act needs no elaboration; the theological significance is what the evangelists narrate.
34: Pater, aphes autois, ou gar oidasin ti poiousin -- "Father, forgive them, for they do not know what they are doing." Textually present in most MSS though absent in some early witnesses (P75, B, D*); Lukan in idiom and theology. Jesus prays for his executioners even as they cast lots. The ignorance motif (ou oidasin) connects to Acts 3:17 ("you acted in ignorance") and Luke 13:27 -- the pattern of uncomprehending guilt that can still receive forgiveness. The soldiers cast lots for his garments (Psalms 22:18 fulfillment).
35: exemykterizou de kai hoi archontes -- "the rulers also sneered" (ekmykterizō = turn the nose up at, mock with contempt). Their taunt: allos esōsen, sōsato heauton = "he saved others, let him save himself." They intended it as mockery; it is Lukan irony -- the very definition of the cross is saving others precisely by not saving himself. Ho Christos tou theou ho eklektos = "the Christ of God, the Chosen One" -- unknowing confession on their lips.
36: The soldiers also mock him, offering him oxos (sour wine/vinegar -- a common soldier's drink), saying "If you are the King of the Jews, save yourself" (ei su ei ho basileus tōn Ioudaiōn, sōson seauton). The triple mockery (rulers v35, soldiers v36-37, one criminal v39) mirrors the triple Pilate verdict in reverse -- there innocence was proclaimed three times, here the title is mocked three times.
37: Ei sy ei ho basileus tōn Ioudaiōn, sōson seauton -- "If you are the King of the Jews, save yourself." The soldiers repeat Pilate's title from the trial. The irony throughout the crucifixion scene is that Jesus is simultaneously mocked as king and is king -- every taunt is an inadvertent confession. His refusal to save himself from the cross is the mechanism of salvation for others (v43 demonstrates this immediately).
38: epigraphē -- the inscription above him. Luke gives the wording simply: Ho basileus tōn Ioudaiōn houtos = "This is the King of the Jews." This titulus (Roman practice of posting the charge) becomes an involuntary proclamation. Pilate's inscription, meant as humiliation, is permanent testimony to Jesus' identity.
39: eblasphēmei auton -- "was blaspheming him." The criminal echoes the rulers' taunt: "Are you not the Christ? Save yourself and us!" -- a self-interested demand for deliverance. Luke's use of blasphēmeō is deliberate: the word used of speaking against God is applied to speaking against Jesus on the cross, equating the two.
40: Ou phobē sy ton theon -- "Do you not fear God?" The second criminal's rebuke begins with the core question of all wisdom literature (fear of God as beginning of wisdom, Proverbs 1:7). Hoti en tō autō krimati ei = "since you are in the same condemnation." Same judgment, same death -- why compound one's dying with mockery of the innocent?
41: Kai hēmeis men dikaios -- "And we indeed justly" -- a remarkable public confession of guilt and acceptance of just punishment. Axios gar hōn epraxamen apolambanomen = "for we are receiving what our deeds deserve." Then the contrast: houtos de ouden atopon epraxen = "but this man has done nothing out of place/wrong." Atopos = "out of place, inappropriate, wrong" -- an understated Greek word that here carries full weight. The dying thief pronounces what the Sanhedrin, Pilate, and Herod all failed to enforce: this man is innocent.
42: Mnēsthēti mou hotan elthēs eis tēn basileian sou -- "Remember me when you come into your kingdom." The grammar is significant: elthēs is an aorist subjunctive -- "whenever you come" -- expressing real expectation of a future arrival. Mnēsthēti = remember me (covenant-memory language in LXX: God "remembered" Noah, Abraham, Hannah). The criminal does not ask for rescue from the cross but for inclusion in Jesus' kingdom. His faith is extraordinary -- he believes in the kingship of a crucified man.
43: Amēn soi legō, sēmeron met' emou esē en tō paradeiso -- "Truly I tell you, today you will be with me in paradise." Sēmeron (today) -- Luke's signature word of eschatological immediacy: Luke 2:11 (born today), Luke 4:21 (fulfilled today), Luke 19:5 (today I must stay at your house), Luke 19:9 (today salvation has come). Even at the cross, salvation breaks through "today." Paradeisos -- a Persian loanword (pairidaeza = walled garden) adopted into Greek via the LXX to translate the Garden of Eden and divine garden imagery. Jewish usage (2 Enoch, 4 Ezra, Testament of Levi) describes paradise as the dwelling of the righteous dead. Jesus promises immediate post-mortem fellowship: met' emou = "with me."
44: ēn de hōsei hōra hektē -- "it was about the sixth hour" (noon). Darkness came over the whole land (skotos egeneto eph' holēn tēn gēn) until the ninth hour (3 pm). Three hours of mid-day darkness recalls the plagues of Egypt (Exodus 10:21-23), the Day of the Lord darkness (Amos 8:9: "I will make the sun go down at noon"), and functions as cosmic mourning/judgment sign at the cross.
45: tou hēliou ekliponto -- "the sun having failed/ceased" (eklipō = to fail, disappear; from this: eclipse). The temple veil torn in two (eschisthē de to katapetasma tou naou meson = "the curtain of the temple was torn down the middle"). The katapetasma was the inner veil separating the Holy of Holies. Its tearing at Jesus' death signals the end of the barrier between God and humanity -- access opened by his sacrifice (cf. Hebrews 10:19-20). Luke places this before Jesus' death (unlike Mark/Matthew), perhaps to frame the death as the cause of the opening.
47: Ontōs ho anthrōpos houtos dikaios ēn -- "Truly this man was righteous." Luke's centurion pronounces Jesus dikaios -- the Greek word carrying both "innocent" (legal innocence) and "righteous" (covenant faithfulness). Mark/Matthew have "Son of God" (Theou Huios); Luke's dikaios fits his emphasis on the judicial vindication of Jesus and connects to the Servant of Isaiah 53:11 ("the righteous one, my servant"). The centurion edoxazen ton theon = "glorified God" -- a Lukan response phrase (Luke 5:25, Luke 13:13, Luke 17:15, Luke 18:43) marking recognition of divine action.
48: theōria -- the spectacle, the sight (theōreō = to watch, contemplate). The crowds who had come for the spectacle (epi tēn theōrian tautēn) witness the events and return home typtomenoi ta stēthē = "beating their breasts." This gesture of grief and contrition (cf. Luke 18:13 -- the tax collector's contrition) suggests the crowd is moved beyond morbid spectacle to something like remorse. Luke's crowd here differs from Mark/Matthew's jeering crowd.
49: heistēkeisan de pantes hoi gnōstoi autō -- "all his acquaintances stood at a distance." Luke adds gnōstoi (known ones, acquaintances) beyond "the women." They stand at a distance, watching (horōsai tauta). The women who had followed from Galilee are specifically named as eyewitnesses -- their unbroken presence from cross to tomb to resurrection is Luke's evidentiary chain for the resurrection narrative.
50: bouleutēs -- a member of the Boulē (council), i.e., the Sanhedrin. Joseph of Arimathea is described as anēr agathos kai dikaios = "a good and righteous man." Dikaios again -- the word used of Simeon (Luke 2:25), Zechariah and Elizabeth (Luke 1:6), John the Baptist (Luke 1:17), and the centurion's verdict. Luke identifies the righteous who surround this death.
51: ou synkatatetheimenos -- "not having consented" -- the perfect participle indicates his ongoing dissent from the Sanhedrin's decision (boulē kai tē praxei autōn = "their counsel and action"). Joseph's righteousness consists partly in what he refused to do. He was waiting expectantly for the kingdom of God (prosdechomenos tēn basileian tou theou) -- the phrase connects him to Simeon (Luke 2:25, Luke 2:38) and marks him as genuinely Israel's hope.
52: prosēlthen tō Pilatō kai ētēsato to sōma tou Iēsou -- Joseph approaches Pilate and asks for Jesus' body. The request required courage (Mark 15:43: tolmēsas); Joseph uses his political access (as council member) for this final act of respect. The body (sōma) would otherwise be left on the cross or thrown in a common grave.
53: sindon -- a linen cloth, fine linen (used for burial wrapping). Joseph wraps the body and places it in a hewn tomb (mnēmati laxeutō = carved/cut from rock) where no one had yet been laid (hou ouk ēn oupō oudeis keimenos = a new, unused tomb). The unused tomb's significance: no confusion of remains is possible; the resurrection will be unambiguous.
54: hēmera paraskeuas -- "day of Preparation," i.e., Friday before Sabbath. Sabbaton epephōsken = "the Sabbath was dawning/beginning to shine." The Jewish day begins at sundown; the Sabbath's approach creates urgency. Joseph completes the burial before sundown, observing Torah timing even in grief.
55: katakolouthsāsai de hai gynaikes -- "the women who had come with him from Galilee followed after." They see the tomb (to mnēmeion) and how his body was laid (hōs etethē to sōma autou). Luke is establishing the chain of witness: the women are eyewitnesses to (1) the crucifixion (v49), (2) the burial location (v55), and (3) later the empty tomb (Luke 24:1-3). Their testimony is foundational.
56: hētoimasan arōmata kai myra -- "they prepared spices and ointments/myrrh" for anointing the body. Then they rested on the Sabbath according to the commandment (kata tēn entolēn). Luke alone adds this phrase of Torah observance -- these women are faithful Jews, resting on Sabbath even in grief, then returning to honor Jesus at the first opportunity (Luke 24:1: "on the first day of the week, very early"). Their Sabbath obedience makes the resurrection morning witness even more carefully timed.
17: anagkēn de eichen apolyein autois kata heotrēn hena -- "For of necessity he must release one to them at the feast." This verse is textually uncertain (absent in P75, Vaticanus, and other early witnesses; present in D, W, and the majority text as a harmonization from Mark 15:6/Matthew 27:15). Whether original or a scribal insertion, the content reflects historical Roman practice of a Passover amnesty. Anagkē = necessity, compulsion -- the same word used of divine necessity elsewhere in Luke, here referring to customary obligation.
